speaker: patient
text: Hi, Doctor. I’ve been feeling really nauseous since yesterday morning.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for coming in. Let’s start with some vitals and labs. Your hemoglobin today is 154 g/L — that’s well within the normal range.

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your fasting glucose, HbA1c, and clinical presentation, I’m diagnosing you with Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus.

speaker: doctor
text: I’d like to start you on Lisinopril 20mg once daily — it’ll help with both blood pressure control and kidney protection given your new diabetes diagnosis.

speaker: patient
text: Okay, I’ll take that as prescribed. Is there anything else I should watch for?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes — monitor for dizziness, especially when standing up, and let me know if you develop a persistent cough or swelling in your face or lips.

speaker: patient
text: Got it. Also, I’ve had a low-grade fever off and on — felt warm this morning.

speaker: doctor
text: Let me check your temperature right now.

speaker: doctor
text: It’s 38.2 °C — that confirms a low-grade fever. Any chills, sore throat, or urinary symptoms?

speaker: doctor
text: Your systolic blood pressure is 94 mmHg — a bit lower than usual. Are you feeling lightheaded?

speaker: patient
text: Yes, a little dizzy when I stand — especially this morning.

speaker: doctor
text: Given the nausea, fever, and borderline hypotension, we should rule out an infection or possible early diabetic ketoacidosis. Let’s check your fingerstick glucose and ketones.

speaker: patient
text: My home glucose was 182 mg/dL before breakfast, but I didn’t test ketones.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s helpful. We’ll run a basic metabolic panel and urinalysis. In the meantime, stay hydrated and hold the Lisinopril today — we’ll reassess dosing once we have more data.

speaker: patient
text: Understood. Should I still take my metformin?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes, continue metformin unless you’re vomiting repeatedly — then pause it and call us.

speaker: patient
text: Actually, I just remembered — that hemoglobin number you mentioned earlier, 154 g/L, matches exactly what my lab report from last week said. So it’s stable.

speaker: doctor
text: Good observation — consistent hemoglobin supports no acute blood loss or hemolysis. Let’s keep monitoring, especially with the fever and nausea.

speaker: patient
text: One more thing — I’ve been checking my blood sugar at home twice daily, and this morning it was 215 mg/dL, which is higher than usual.

speaker: doctor
text: Given the intercurrent illness, I’m going to switch you temporarily to insulin glargine 10 units at bedtime to improve glycemic control while we address the underlying cause of your symptoms.
